note: Structured monthly summaries of the ONS public sector finances release. The newest entry is shown first and drives the monthly explainer page. Add a new object to the top of 'updates' each month.

updates.source_name: ONS, Public sector finances, UK: June 2026
updates.headline: Borrowing in June was sharply lower than a year earlier and slightly below the official forecast, helped by cheaper debt interest.

answers.whatChanged: The government borrowed £16.0 billion in June 2026, the gap between what it spent and what it raised. This was £7.9 billion less than the same month last year and slightly below the OBR forecast.
answers.borrowingWhy: Tax receipts grew strongly while debt interest costs fell sharply, more than offsetting higher spending on public services and benefits.
answers.taxReceipts: Receipts grew strongly over the year, led by income tax, corporation tax and VAT.
answers.spending: Debt interest fell sharply, but spending on public services, benefits and other costs rose, leaving overall spending slightly lower than a year earlier.
answers.debtInterest: Central government debt interest payable was £11.8 billion in June, £5.3 billion less than a year earlier, as the inflation uplift on index-linked gilts was far smaller than in June 2025.
answers.vsObr: Borrowing came in £0.3 billion below the OBR's forecast for the month, largely because of lower inflation-linked debt interest costs, though one month is a weak guide and figures are routinely revised.

updates.source_name: ONS, Public sector finances, UK: May 2026
updates.headline: Borrowing in May was higher than a year earlier and well above the official forecast, the second-highest May on record.

borrowing.vsForecast: £5.6 billion above the OBR forecast of £17.7 billion

answers.whatChanged: The government borrowed £23.3 billion in May 2026, the gap between what it spent and what it raised. This was up on the same month last year and well above the OBR forecast.
answers.borrowingWhy: Spending grew faster than receipts, with a sharp rise in debt interest the biggest single pressure.
answers.taxReceipts: Receipts grew over the year, helped by VAT, income tax and corporation tax, but not enough to offset higher spending.
answers.spending: Day-to-day spending rose, including on debt interest, departmental services and benefits.
answers.debtInterest: Central government debt interest payable was £11.7 billion in May, the highest for any May on record, largely reflecting inflation uplift on index-linked gilts.
answers.vsObr: Borrowing came in £5.6 billion above the OBR's forecast of £17.7 billion for the month, though one month is a weak guide and figures are routinely revised.

updates.source_name: ONS, Public sector finances, UK: April 2026
updates.headline: Borrowing in April was higher than a year earlier and above the official forecast, the second-highest April on record.

borrowing.vsForecast: £3.4 billion above the OBR forecast of £20.9 billion

answers.whatChanged: The government borrowed £24.3 billion in April 2026, the gap between what it spent and what it raised. This was up on the same month last year and higher than the OBR had forecast.
answers.borrowingWhy: Spending grew faster than receipts, with higher debt interest and benefit costs among the pressures.
answers.taxReceipts: Receipts grew over the year, helped by income tax and National Insurance, but not enough to offset higher spending.
answers.spending: Day-to-day spending rose, including on benefits and public services.
answers.debtInterest: Central government debt interest payable was £10.3 billion in April, the highest for any April on record, reflecting inflation uplift on index-linked gilts and higher interest rates.
answers.vsObr: Borrowing came in above the OBR's March forecast for the month, an early sign the year may run higher than planned, though one month is a weak guide and figures are routinely revised.
